name: Crocea Mors
description: Crocea Mors, meaning 'Yellow Death' or 'Yellow Sword', is the legendary sword of Julius Caesar, according to Geoffrey of Monmouth's fictional history, Historia Regum Britanniae (History of the Kings of Britain). It is said to have been brought to Britain during Caesar's invasion. The sword is described as being incredibly sharp and deadly, capable of inflicting wounds that quickly became fatal. It was later wielded by Nennius, a British chieftain who fought against Caesar. Nennius is said to have stolen the sword from Caesar during a fierce battle, dealing a fatal blow to the Roman commander's shield or helmet. However, the blow also wounded Nennius, and he died fifteen days later, ordering the sword buried with him. The act of taking the sword from Caesar is viewed as a symbolic victory for the Britons, demonstrating their resistance against the Roman Empire. While the historical accuracy of the sword's existence and the events surrounding it is highly questionable, Crocea Mors has become an important symbol of British resistance and a significant artifact within British mythology and Arthurian legend, often appearing in subsequent tales as a powerful weapon of great renown and historical importance. The sword represents both the brutality of war and the defiance of a people against oppression. Its legacy lives on as a reminder of the battles fought and the sacrifices made in the name of freedom. The association with Caesar adds to the sword's mystique, linking it to the vast Roman Empire and imbuing it with a sense of grandeur and power.
subtitle: The Yellow Death of Julius Caesar
origin: Forged for or obtained by Julius Caesar

keyMyths.title: Caesar's Invasion
keyMyths.description: Crocea Mors appears during Caesar's invasion of Britain and is wielded by him. Nennius later steals the sword.
keyMyths.source: Geoffrey of Monmouth, Historia Regum Britanniae

keyMyths.title: Nennius' Death
keyMyths.description: Nennius dies from a wound inflicted while stealing Crocea Mors from Caesar. He requests the sword be buried with him.
keyMyths.source: Geoffrey of Monmouth, Historia Regum Britanniae

sources.author: Geoffrey of Monmouth
sources.description: Fictional history of the Kings of Britain
sources.text: Fictional history of the Kings of Britain
